title: Setting up the pole lathe: drive centre and dead centre
subtitle: Mounting a billet correctly between the drive and dead centres
excerpt: The drive centre and dead centre are the two points that hold the billet on the pole lathe. Getting the mounting right; centred on the billet axis, drive centre fully engaged, dead centre lubricated; determines the quality of every turning that follows. This technique guide covers centre-finding, mounting, and the first two cuts to confirm the billet is running true.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for pole-lathe setup and turning technique; Roy Underhill The Woodwright's Shop (1981) referenced for technique authority, not reproduced.

content.text: The pole lathe is a reciprocating lathe: the treadle cord wraps once around the billet, and on the down-stroke the billet rotates into the cutting tool; on the up-stroke the pole spring returns the treadle and the billet rotates back the other way. Cutting happens only on the down-stroke. The billet is held between the
drive centre
at the headstock and the
dead centre
at the tailstock. The mounting must be centred and firm; an off-centre
billet
will wobble, vibrate, and eject from the lathe under the treadle load.
Eye protection: wear safety glasses when turning. Green-wood chips are wet and fly at speed. A chip in the eye is a serious injury.

Billet security: before cutting, rotate the billet by hand through a full revolution to confirm it is seated on both centres without wobble. A wobbling billet must be re-centred before turning begins.
Tool rest: the tool rest must be firm and positioned so the turning gouge has a stable surface to ride on. An unstable rest allows the gouge to dig in.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach.
When to use this technique

This setup sequence is the start of every turning session on the pole lathe; stool legs, chair rungs, knife handles, dibbers, tops. The first two cuts described at the end of this guide; the ghost cut and the first taper cut; are confirmation steps that apply to every new billet, regardless of the final form.
Step-by-step
Prepare the billet: rough the
green

billet to an approximate cylinder at the shaving horse with the drawknife, or use the axe for a short heavy billet. A round cross-section is not required; a faceted cylinder from drawknife work is fine. The billet must be straight along its axis; a curved billet will not run true.
Find the centre of each end face: draw two diagonal lines from corner to corner of the roughly-square end grain. The intersection is the centre. Mark it with a bradawl or the tip of the sloyd knife.

Drive the drive-centre spurs into the headstock end face with a mallet: two or three beetle-weight blows. The spurs must be embedded 4 to 5 mm deep. A spur that sits at 2 mm will pull out under the treadle load; the billet will spin on the drive centre without rotating.

Apply tallow, beeswax, or a small daub of soap to the dead-centre point and to the corresponding dimple in the tailstock end face of the billet. The dead centre does not rotate; friction between the stationary dead centre and the rotating billet will generate heat. Lubrication reduces that friction; without it the dead-centre dimple can char and seize.

Slide the tailstock to bring the dead centre against the dimple. Apply light pressure and lock the tailstock. The billet should be held firmly but not clamped; some axial movement is acceptable; radial movement is not.

The ghost cut: with the treadle cord wrapped around the billet, press the treadle down slowly and hold the turning tool away from the work. Observe the billet as it rotates. A round billet that is well-centred shows no wobble. A billet with the drive centre off-axis shows a wobble that can be seen at the ends.

The first taper cut: rest the turning gouge on the tool rest with its edge 3 mm from the billet surface. On the next treadle down-stroke, advance the gouge until it catches the high spots of the rotating billet; the cut will be intermittent at first, catching only the largest facets. After 4 to 5 strokes the billet will be running as a true cylinder.
What it looks like when it is right

A correctly mounted billet shows no visible wobble in the ghost cut; the silhouette of the rotating billet is sharp rather than blurred. On the first taper cuts, shavings lift cleanly from the whole circumference within 6 to 8 treadle strokes. The dead-centre dimple remains cool to the touch after 10 minutes of turning; it should not be warm, and it should not smoke.
Common mistakes
Drive centre not fully seated: the spur slips and the billet spins without rotating the cord. Drive the centre spur in to at least 4 mm.
Dead centre not lubricated: the dimple chars. Stop, remove the billet, re-wax the dead centre, and recheck the dimple for depth before continuing.
Centre line drawn with a ruler from the corners of a non-square end grain: the true centre of a round billet end grain is found with dividers, not a ruler.
